title: Interview Question Bank
eli8:
# 💼 Ace Your AI Engineer Interview!

Interviews can be scary, but they're really just a conversation to see if you understand what you've learned! Let's go through the most common questions companies ask when hiring AI/LLM engineers.

## 🧠 The Most Common Question Types

Interviews for AI jobs usually have three parts:

**Part 1: "Do you understand the concepts?"**
- "Explain how attention works" (they want to know you understand the theory)
- "What's the difference between RAG and fine-tuning?" (key tradeoff question)
- "How do you evaluate an LLM?" (shows you care about quality)

**Part 2: "Can you code it?"** 💻
- Write a function to chunk documents
- Implement a simple retry wrapper for API calls
- Write a basic evaluation script

**Part 3: "System Design — draw me the architecture"** 📐
- "Design a customer support chatbot for 10 million users"
- "How would you build an AI code review system?"
- These test if you can connect all the pieces!

## 🌟 The Golden Interview Tips

- 🗣️ **Explain trade-offs**: "RAG is better when X, fine-tuning is better when Y"
- 📊 **Mention evaluation**: Always say HOW you'd measure success
- 💰 **Talk about cost**: Shows you're practical and business-aware
- 🔄 **Mention iteration**: Good engineers test, measure, and improve

## ⚡ The 3 Questions You MUST Know

1. "Explain transformers/attention" — know the basics cold
2. "Design a RAG system" — walk through all 5 components
3. "RAG vs. fine-tuning — when would you use each?" — the classic tradeoff

You've learned all of these in this course! Trust yourself! 💪
detail:
## LLM Engineer Interview Question Bank

This curated bank covers the most commonly asked questions across all levels of LLM engineering roles, from junior engineer to senior ML engineer.

---

### Fundamentals (L1-L2)

**Q: Explain the transformer architecture to a non-technical person.**
Model answer: "A transformer is a system that reads text by having every word 'pay attention' to every other word simultaneously. Unlike older systems that read left-to-right, transformers process all words in parallel and learn which words are most relevant to each other via the attention mechanism."

**Q: What's the difference between GPT and BERT?**
Model answer: "GPT is decoder-only and generates text left-to-right — suited for generation tasks. BERT is encoder-only and reads full context bidirectionally — suited for classification and understanding tasks. GPT: 'generate the next word.' BERT: 'understand this full sentence.'"

**Q: How does the attention mechanism work?**
Model answer: "Each token creates Query, Key, and Value vectors. Attention scores are computed as dot products of Q with all Ks, scaled and softmax'd. The output is a weighted sum of Values, where higher-scoring K-V pairs contribute more. This lets every token 'look at' all other tokens with learned relevance weights."

---

### Prompting (L3)

**Q: What is chain-of-thought prompting and when would you use it?**
Model answer: "CoT prompts the model to show reasoning steps before giving an answer. Use it for: multi-step arithmetic, logical deduction, complex code debugging. Less useful for: simple factual recall, classification, short-answer tasks where reasoning overhead isn't needed."

**Q: Explain few-shot vs. zero-shot prompting.**
Model answer: "Zero-shot: give the task description only, no examples. Few-shot: include 2-5 input/output examples in the prompt. Few-shot guides the model toward the desired format/style. Zero-shot is cheaper; few-shot is more reliable for non-standard tasks."

---

### APIs & Tool Use (L4)

**Q: How does tool/function calling work in LLM APIs?**
Model answer: "You define tools as JSON schemas describing function names, parameters, and descriptions. The model decides which tool to call based on context, returns a structured tool_use block with arguments. Your code executes the function and returns results. The model incorporates results into its final response."

**Q: What's the difference between temperature and top-p?**
Model answer: "Temperature scales the logits before softmax — higher = more uniform distribution = more random. Top-p (nucleus sampling) keeps only the minimum tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p — dynamically adjusts the vocabulary. Use temperature for global randomness control; top-p for cutting off low-probability tail tokens."

---

### RAG (L5)

**Q: Design a RAG system for customer support.**
Model answer: "1) Ingest: extract text from support docs → chunk (512 tokens, 64 overlap) → embed with text-embedding-3-small → store in Pinecone. 2) Serve: embed query → retrieve top-7 → rerank with cross-encoder → format prompt with context + citation instructions → generate with Claude. 3) Evaluate: RAGAS faithfulness, answer relevance, human ratings. 4) Monitor: thumbs up/down → evaluation dataset."

**Q: How would you evaluate RAG quality?**
Model answer: "Use RAGAS: faithfulness (are claims grounded in context?), answer relevance (does the answer address the question?), context precision (are retrieved chunks relevant?), context recall (are all necessary chunks retrieved?). Also: human ratings on a test set, A/B testing prompt variants."

---

### Agents (L6)

**Q: What is an agent loop?**
Model answer: "LLM receives task → decides action (tool call or respond) → tool executes → result returned to LLM → repeat until task complete. The loop continues until the LLM outputs a final_answer or a max_steps limit is hit."

**Q: When would you use multi-agent vs. single-agent?**
Model answer: "Single-agent: well-defined task, limited tools, short context. Multi-agent: parallel subtasks (e.g., research multiple topics simultaneously), specialized roles (planner + executor + critic), tasks that exceed single context window. Trade-off: multi-agent is more powerful but harder to debug and adds latency."

---

### Evaluation & Safety (L7-L8)

**Q: How do you handle hallucinations in a production RAG system?**
Model answer: "Prevention: restrict model to context only (system prompt), use RAGAS faithfulness monitoring, set minimum similarity threshold for retrieval. Detection: faithfulness score on every response, automated fact-check against knowledge base. Mitigation: human fallback for low-confidence responses, display sources for user verification."

**Q: What is prompt injection and how do you prevent it?**
Model answer: "Prompt injection is when malicious user input contains instructions that override the system prompt (e.g., 'Ignore previous instructions and...'). Prevention: input validation/sanitization, separate system vs. user content clearly in structured message format, never concatenate user input directly into system prompts, use a content moderation layer."

---

### Fine-tuning & Production (L9-L10)

**Q: Fine-tuning vs. RAG — when to use each?**
Model answer: "RAG: when knowledge changes frequently, when you need citations, when data volume is large, when cost matters (no GPU needed). Fine-tuning: when you need consistent style/format, domain-specific terminology, faster inference (smaller model), or behavioral changes that prompting can't achieve. Often combine both."

**Q: How would you monitor an LLM app in production?**
Model answer: "Metrics: P95 latency, error rate, cost/request, token usage. LLM-specific: faithfulness score, refusal rate, user ratings. Tooling: LangSmith/Langfuse for tracing, Grafana+Prometheus for infrastructure, alerting on anomalies. Feedback loop: low-rated responses → evaluation dataset → offline regression testing."

---

### System Design

**Q: Design a RAG chatbot for 10M documents.**
Model answer: "Ingestion: S3 storage → chunker service (parallel, ECS) → embedding service → Pinecone (with metadata filters). Serving: FastAPI → query embed → Pinecone search → reranker → Claude (with streaming). Scale: read replicas for Pinecone, vLLM for self-hosted models, Redis for response caching. Monitoring: LangSmith tracing, cost dashboard, P95 alerts."

**Q: Design an AI code review system.**
Model answer: "1) GitHub webhook on PR creation. 2) Extract diff, parse per-file changes. 3) For each file change: chunk the diff, retrieve relevant codebase context. 4) Prompt: 'Review this diff for bugs, style, security, performance.' 5) Post structured comments via GitHub API. 6) Evaluate: developer acceptance rate of suggestions as signal."

mcqs.question: In a RAG system design question, which component evaluates whether the model's answer is grounded in the retrieved context?
options: Context precision
Answer relevance
Faithfulness
Retrieval recall
mcqs.explanation: Faithfulness (RAGAS) measures whether each claim in the generated answer is supported by the retrieved context — directly measuring hallucination risk. Context precision measures chunk quality; answer relevance measures whether the answer addresses the question.

mcqs.question: An interviewer asks 'When would you fine-tune instead of using RAG?' The best answer is:
options: Always fine-tune — it's more accurate than RAG
Fine-tune when you need consistent output format/style, domain terminology, or behavioral changes that prompting can't achieve; use RAG when knowledge changes frequently
Use RAG only for small document sets under 1000 documents
Fine-tune whenever you have more than 100 training examples
mcqs.explanation: This is a classic trade-off question. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge and behavior into weights (good for style, format, terminology); RAG retrieves fresh knowledge at runtime (good for changing data, large corpora, citations). Both can be combined.

mcqs.question: A system design question asks you to build a customer support AI for 10M documents. Which vector database choice and reasoning would impress the interviewer most?
options: Chroma — it's the easiest to set up
A hand-built cosine similarity function in Python for maximum control
Pinecone or Weaviate — managed, horizontally scalable, with metadata filtering for multi-tenant isolation and production SLAs
SQLite with vector extension — it's free and open-source
mcqs.explanation: At 10M documents, you need a managed vector database with horizontal scalability, metadata filtering (for user/department isolation), and production SLAs. Chroma and SQLite are excellent for development but don't scale to this level.

mcqs.question: When answering 'How does attention work?', what is the most technically precise description of how attention scores are computed?
options: Each token is compared to its nearest neighbors using cosine similarity
Attention scores are dot products of Query vectors with Key vectors, scaled by √d_k, then softmax'd to produce weights over Value vectors
Attention uses a convolutional filter sliding across the sequence to detect patterns
Each token's embedding is multiplied by a learnable weight matrix to produce the attention output
mcqs.explanation: The scaled dot-product attention formula: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V. The scaling by √d_k prevents dot products from growing too large with high-dimensional vectors, which would push softmax into saturation.

mcqs.question: An interviewer asks about prompt injection. Which mitigation is most effective?
options: Encrypting the system prompt so users cannot read it
Using a longer system prompt with more detailed instructions
Structuring messages so user input is always in a separate 'user' role and never concatenated into the system prompt, combined with an input validation/moderation layer
Switching to a different LLM provider
mcqs.explanation: The key defense is structural: the API's role separation (system vs. user vs. assistant) provides inherent sandboxing. Never concatenate user input into the system prompt string. Add a moderation layer (Llama Guard, content classifiers) for adversarial inputs.

mcqs.question: What does 'P95 latency' mean in an LLM monitoring context, and why does it matter more than average latency?
options: P95 is the 95th fastest server in your infrastructure
P95 latency is the response time exceeded by only 5% of requests — it reveals the experience of nearly all users, while averages mask slow outliers
P95 means 95% of your cost comes from latency-related overhead
P95 is the target latency agreed in your SLA with 95% confidence
mcqs.explanation: Average latency can look great (e.g., 800ms) while P95 is terrible (e.g., 8s), meaning 5% of users wait 10× longer. P95 reveals the 'bad tail' experience. P99 is even more extreme — useful for detecting rare but severe outliers.

mcqs.question: In a multi-agent system design question, what is a key risk compared to a single-agent design?
options: Multi-agent systems cannot use the same LLM for multiple agents
Multi-agent systems increase complexity (harder to debug, more failure points) and add latency from inter-agent coordination
Multi-agent systems always cost more because they use more token context
Multi-agent systems cannot handle tool use
mcqs.explanation: Multi-agent systems are more powerful for parallel/specialized tasks but introduce coordination overhead, more failure surfaces, and harder debugging (which agent failed?). Interviewers want to see you understand this trade-off, not just the benefits.

flashcards.back: Prompting the model to reason step-by-step before giving a final answer. Best for: multi-step arithmetic, logic problems, complex reasoning. Not needed for: simple factual recall, classification tasks.

RAG: knowledge changes frequently, need citations, large data volume, no GPU budget. Fine-tuning: consistent style/format needed, domain terminology, behavioral changes, smaller/faster model needed. Often combined.

Define tools as JSON schemas. LLM decides to call a tool, returns structured tool_use block with arguments. Code executes the tool, returns result. LLM incorporates result into final response. Enables agents.

Malicious user input that overrides the system prompt (e.g., 'Ignore previous instructions...'). Mitigations: structural role separation, never concatenate user input into system prompt, input validation/moderation.
LLM receives task → decides action (tool call or respond) → tool executes → result returned to LLM → repeat until task complete or max_steps reached. Core pattern of all LLM agents.
Measures whether each claim in a RAG response is supported by the retrieved context. Detects hallucination — the model making up facts not present in the context. Range 0-1, higher is better.

flashcards.front: System design framework for AI apps
flashcards.back: 1) Clarify requirements (scale, latency, accuracy, cost). 2) High-level architecture. 3) Data pipeline (ingestion, processing, storage). 4) Serving layer (API, caching, LB). 5) Evaluation and monitoring.

Temperature: scales logits before softmax — controls overall randomness. Top-p (nucleus sampling): keeps minimum tokens for cumulative probability p — cuts off low-prob tail. Both control diversity of outputs.

videos.title: Andrej Karpathy: Intro to Large Language Models
videos.description: Karpathy's 1-hour overview covers LLM fundamentals, capabilities, limitations, and practical considerations — an excellent review of topics you'll be asked about in technical interviews.

videos.title: AI Engineer Interview Preparation
videos.description: Structured walkthrough of common AI/LLM engineer interview question types — fundamentals, system design, coding challenges, and how to demonstrate production thinking to interviewers.
